diagrams.title: DAO Architecture and Components
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing the complete architecture of a Decentralized Autonomous Organization including blockchain layer, smart contracts, governance tokens, and member interfaces
elements: Blockchain Layer - Ethereum/Polygon/Solana providing immutable ledger and consensus
Smart Contracts - Governance contracts, Treasury contracts, Proposal contracts, Voting contracts
Governance Token - ERC-20 token representing voting rights and ownership
Member Interface - Web3 wallets (MetaMask), DAO dashboards (DAOhaus, Aragon)
Treasury - Multi-sig wallet holding funds and executing approved decisions
Proposal System - Idea submission, discussion, voting queue, execution
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete technical stack of a DAO and how each component interacts to achieve decentralization without traditional hierarchical management

diagrams.title: DAO Decision Making and Voting Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart illustrating the complete lifecycle of a proposal in a DAO from idea generation to execution
elements: Member submits proposal with required parameters
Proposal enters discussion period for community feedback
Voting period opens with quorum requirements specified
Token holders cast votes (For/Against/Abstain)
Vote tallying based on token-weighted or quadratic voting
If quorum met and majority achieved → Proposal passes
Proposal queued for timelock period (security delay)
Execution via smart contract interaction
Results recorded on blockchain - immutable audit trail
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the democratic, transparent governance mechanism of DAOs and understand how decisions are made collectively without central authority

diagrams.title: Comparison: Traditional Organization vs DAO
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison block diagram showing structural differences between conventional companies and DAOs
elements: Traditional: Hierarchical management with CEO/Board | DAO: Flat structure with token-holder governance
Traditional: Centralized decision making | DAO: Decentralized voting-based decisions
Traditional: Employees with employment contracts | DAO: Contributors with token incentives
Traditional: Bank accounts, legal entities | DAO: Crypto wallets, smart contracts
Traditional: Private internal records | DAO: Public blockchain transactions
Traditional: Slow manual processes | DAO: Automated smart contract execution
Traditional: Geographical restrictions | DAO: Global permissionless participation
diagrams.purpose: Students understand key advantages of DAOs over traditional organizations including transparency, global accessibility, and automation

diagrams.title: DAO Smart Contract Structure
diagrams.description: Technical diagram showing the relationship between different smart contracts that power a DAO
elements: Governance Contract - Stores proposals, voting logic, member registry
Token Contract - ERC-20/ERC-721 implementing voting power and ownership
Treasury Contract - Manages funds, handles deposits/withdrawals, multi-sig execution
Executor Contract - Interfaces with external protocols, executes passed proposals
Timelock Controller - Security mechanism delaying execution after voting approval
Extension Contracts - Plugin architecture for adding new features
diagrams.purpose: Students gain technical understanding of how DAOs are implemented at the smart contract level and the modular design approach

diagrams.title: DAO Ecosystem and Stakeholder Roles
diagrams.description: Diagram showing the various participants and their roles in a DAO ecosystem
elements: Token Holders - Vote on proposals, delegate voting power, receive rewards
Developers - Build and maintain smart contracts, protocol upgrades
Community Managers - Engage members, moderate discussions, coordinate initiatives
Treasury Managers - Manage funds, propose budget allocations, handle multi-sig
Validators/Node Operators - Secure the underlying blockchain network
External Services - Oracles (Chainlink), Indexers (The Graph), Storage (IPFS)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the diverse roles within a DAO ecosystem and how different stakeholders contribute to decentralized governance

charts.title: DAO Governance Models Comparison
charts.content: QUORUM-BASED: Simple majority vote | Quadratic Voting: Reduces whale influence through square root calculation | Conviction Voting: Time-weighted voting where longer locks = more influence | Rage Quit: Exit with proportional treasury share anytime | Delegated: Token holders delegate votes to representatives | Holographic: DAO can spawn sub-DAOs for scaling governance

charts.title: DAO Funding and Treasury Mechanisms
charts.content: TOKEN SALE: Initial token distribution to raise funds | TREASURY DIVERSIFICATION: Converting holdings to stablecoins | GRANTS PROGRAM: Funding external developers and projects | BOUNTIES: Paying for specific deliverables | STAKING REWARDS: Income from protocol tokens held | NFT SALES: Revenue from digital asset minting | FEE GENERATION: Protocol fees distributed to token holders

charts.title: DAO Creation and Launch Process
charts.content: 1. Define Purpose → 2. Draft Governance Documents (Constitution) → 3. Deploy Smart Contracts → 4. Distribute Governance Tokens → 5. Set Initial Parameters (Quorum, Voting Period) → 6. Assign Admin Roles (Temporary) → 7. Launch Community → 8. Transfer Control to Token Holders → 9. Ongoing Governance

charts.title: Types of DAOs by Purpose
charts.content: PROTOCOL DAOs: Govern DeFi protocols (Uniswap, MakerDAO) | INVESTMENT DAOs: Collective investment in startups/crypto (BitDAO) | GRANT DAOs: Fund ecosystem development (Gitcoin) | SOCIAL DAOs: Community-focused with membership NFTs (Friends with Benefits) | MEDIA DAOs: Decentralized content creation (BanklessDAO) | PHILANTHROPY DAOs: Charitable giving (The Giving Block)

branches: Definition: Blockchain-based organizations governed by smart contracts and token-holder voting
Core Principles: Decentralization, Transparency, Autonomy, Collective Ownership
Key Features: Voting mechanisms, Treasury management, Proposal systems, Token governance
Smart Contracts: Governance, Token, Treasury, Executor contracts
Governance Models: Quadratic, Conviction, Delegated, Token-weighted voting
Advantages: Global participation, 24/7 voting, Immutable records, No intermediaries
Challenges: Legal uncertainty, Whale dominance, Smart contract risks, Coordination costs
Real Examples: MakerDAO, Uniswap, BitDAO, ConstitutionDAO, ENS DAO
Ecosystem Tools: Snapshot, Tally, DAOhaus, Aragon, Safe (Multi-sig)
Future Trends: Legal wrapper integration, Cross-chain DAOs, DAO-to-DAO collaboration
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of DAOs including technical implementation, governance models, real-world applications, and challenges for exam preparation
